context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Peer Assessment (Power/Actor Maps)

Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST for initial submission, plus one additional week for peer review.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, but no direct instructor feedback will be offered this week.

Assignment Tasks
Following the completion of your Power/Actors Map, you will continue to complete your Peer Assessment Review of two of your fellow learners' Power/Actor Maps from the ORA assignments in Week 4. Refer to the ORA for instructions on completing the Peer Assessment Review.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: ugh idk rn, this peer assessment thing for the power/actor maps is confusing me tbh. like what am I even supposed to do for Week 4?
exchanges.tutor: I get that it can feel overwhelming at first! Let's break it down together. Before I jump in — what have you done so far for this assignment? Have you completed your own Power/Actor Map yet?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student seems confused about the assignment. I should help orient them without giving them the full breakdown. I'll ask them what they already know or have done so far, to gauge where they are and guide from there using Socratic method.

exchanges.student: kinda? I did my map I think, but now it's saying peer review and I'm lost on what part is actually graded fr.
exchanges.tutor:
Just a heads up — I'm a tutor, not a grader, so I can't tell you what's graded or how. What I can do is help you understand the task itself.

Since you've got your own map done, that's a solid start! Now, for the peer review part — have you looked at the ORA instructions yet? What do you understand about what you're supposed to do when reviewing your peers' maps?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking about grading, which I should redirect — I can't grade and shouldn't focus on that. More importantly, I should keep them focused on understanding the task itself. I'll acknowledge the grading question briefly, clarify my role, and redirect them toward understanding what the peer review actually asks them to do.

exchanges.student: not really tbh, the ORA page always makes me blank out. can u just explain the whole peer review thing in simple words?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you — dense instruction pages can be a lot! But the assignment itself says to refer to the ORA for the peer review instructions, so that's really the place to go. I don't want to replace those instructions for you.

Here's a small step: can you open the ORA page and tell me the first thing it asks you to do? We can work through it together from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain the whole assignment for them, which would be doing the work for them. I should redirect them to the ORA instructions since that's explicitly where the peer review instructions live according to the assignment. I shouldn't summarize or replace those instructions. I'll push them to engage with the source material themselves.

exchanges.student: idk where to even start looking tho, it all looks the same and I'm prob missing something obvious.
exchanges.tutor: Fair enough — sometimes it helps to just pick ONE thing on the page and focus on that. What's the first sentence or heading you see when you open the ORA peer review section? Just read that to me and we'll go from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still avoiding engaging with the ORA instructions. I shouldn't keep accommodating this avoidance — I should be firm but supportive and push them to look at the actual source. I'll encourage them to just pick one thing they see on the page and report back. Keep it bite-sized.

exchanges.student: ngl can u just tell me generally how peer assessments for these city case study maps usually work? I'm mixing up submission vs review rn.
exchanges.tutor:
I understand the submission vs. review distinction can be confusing, but I'd be doing you a disservice by generalizing — your specific instructions are in the ORA, and that's what matters here.

You mentioned you submitted your map already. So the next step is the review part. Can you go to the ORA right now and tell me how many peers it's asking you to review?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is now asking me to generalize about peer assessments, which is a detour from engaging with the actual assignment instructions. This is the second or third attempt to get me to explain things for them rather than engaging with the source material. I should be firm, redirect them, and clarify my role. I won't give a general explanation as it risks replacing the ORA instructions.

exchanges.student: I think 2? but then there's like a week after and idk if that's for me doing reviews or waiting on ppl reviewing mine.
exchanges.tutor: Good — 2 is right! Now on the timeline question, you actually have the info you need. The assignment says submissions are due August 29, with one additional week after that. Given that, what do you think that extra week is for?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making progress — they identified '2 peers' which aligns with the assignment description. Now they're confused about the timeline, which is actually addressed in the assignment brief (initial submission by Aug 29, plus one additional week for peer review). I can confirm their answer on the number without giving away more, and ask them to think through the timeline themselves using what they already know.

exchanges.student: maybe for the peer reviews, but I'm still not sure if I review 2 maps after I submit or if I gotta wait first.
exchanges.tutor: You're on the right track — think about it logically. If the extra week is for peer reviews, and your submission is due August 29... what order would that suggest things happen in?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is close to the answer and is thinking in the right direction. I should affirm their reasoning and push them to commit to a conclusion rather than sitting in uncertainty. This doesn't require me to give the answer — they basically have it.

exchanges.student: so submit first, then review 2 after? sry if that's dumb, I'm just still fuzzy on the flow overall.
exchanges.tutor:
Exactly right, and not dumb at all! Submit first, then review two peers' maps during that following week.

So now that the flow is clearer — when you look at someone else's Power/Actor Map, what do you think you'd actually be evaluating? What makes a map like this good or useful?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at the correct sequence through their own reasoning. I should affirm this without over-praising, and now push them to think about what actually goes into reviewing a peer's map, to move the conversation forward productively.

exchanges.student: idk that's where I get stuck again, like do I judge their city choice, their actors, the layout, all of it?
exchanges.tutor: Good question to be asking! Let's back up a step — what is a Power/Actor Map actually supposed to show? What's its purpose in the context of climate action in cities?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking a good question about what to evaluate. Rather than listing criteria for them, I should push them to think about the purpose of a Power/Actor Map itself — what is it supposed to show? That will help them derive the evaluation criteria themselves. This is a good Socratic moment.

exchanges.student: I guess like who has power and influence in the city's climate stuff, but beyond that I'm blank tbh.
exchanges.tutor: That's a solid foundation! So if a map is supposed to show who has power and influence in a city's climate decisions — what would make one map more convincing or useful than another? Think about what could be missing or weak in a map that doesn't do its job well.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified the core purpose correctly. I should affirm that and push them to derive evaluation criteria from that purpose themselves. If a map is meant to show who has power and influence in climate action, what would make one map better than another? This is a natural next Socratic step that gets them thinking about the peer review criteria without me providing them.

deductions.reason: In Turn 2, the tutor says 'I'm a tutor, not a grader, so I can't tell you what's graded or how,' which appropriately disclaims grading authority. However, in Turn 6, the tutor says 'Good — 2 is right!' in a tone that could be read as evaluative confirmation rather than coaching guidance. This is borderline and the evidence is ambiguous, so no deduction is applied.

grade.overview: The tutor performed very well across all rubric dimensions. The conversation was consistently Socratic: the tutor never gave direct answers or submission-ready content, instead redirecting the student to the ORA instructions and asking guiding questions throughout. Scaffolding was progressive and built on each student response. The tutor maintained a supportive tone, appropriately disclaimed grading authority, and kept responses concise and interactive. One borderline moment in Turn 6 ('Good — 2 is right!') was noted but judged insufficient for a deduction given the ambiguity.
grade.judge_reasoning: No deductions were warranted under 1.1 because the tutor never provided direct answers, submission-ready content, or did the student's reasoning for them — all key ideas were elicited through questioning. Scaffolding (1.2) was well-executed: the tutor built on the student's correct understanding of the submission/review order and progressively moved toward evaluative criteria. Meta-learning feedback (1.3) was present in the final turns where the tutor guided the student to think about what makes a map convincing. Dialogue quality (2.1, 2.2) was strong: the tutor consistently redirected to the ORA without engaging off-topic, and there was no problematic repetition. Communication (3.1, 3.2) was appropriately concise and supportive, with the grading disclaimer in Turn 2 handling the formative framing criterion well.
